text: The robotic surgical system is used to repair a right indirect inguinal hernia, with robotic arms maneuvering instruments to manipulate and suture tissue in the inguinal region.
The robotic camera provides a clear view of the medial and lateral leaflets of the peritoneal flap, which converge at the junction with the inferior epigastric vessels.
The surgeon navigates into the supravesicular space on the medial portion after crossing the junction of the medial and lateral leaflets of the peritoneal flap near the inferior epigastric vessels.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to grasp the hernia sac at an oblique angle during the robotic repair of a right indirect inguinal hernia.

The surgeon adjusts the oblique angle of the instrument while pulling up out of the trocar, maintaining close proximity to the peritoneal flap during the robotic repair of a right indirect inguinal hernia.
The surgeon uses cautery to apply a few pulses, then manipulates the cord lipoma downward using the peritoneal flap and hernia sac.
The surgeon continues to work on the peritoneal flap, carefully separating the surrounding fat and spermatic vessels from the flap using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the vas deferens, maintaining close proximity without causing injury to the deferential vessel.
The surgeon uses the cautery device to apply a few pulses around the corner of the surgical field, nearing the completion of the procedure.
The surgeon pulls the indirect sac down, focusing on the medial part of the dissection as they progress past the symphysis pubis.
The surgeon performs high lateral fixation using two ovicral sutures, securing the mesh in place.
The surgeon ties a granny knot on the pubic tubercle and uses a Marilyn dissector to gently secure the knot in place.
The surgeon identifies the medial branches of the inferior epigastric vessels, noting their potential for significant bleeding during the procedure.
